coredata.dc:description: Diabetic wounds present significant treatment challenges due to their complex microenvironment, marked by persistent inflammation from bacterial infections, hypoxia caused by diabetic microangiopathy, and biofilm colonization. Sonodynamic therapy (SDT) offers potential for treating such wounds by targeting deep tissues with antibacterial effects, but its efficacy is limited by hypoxic conditions and biofilm barriers. To overcome these obstacles, we developed a novel approach using oxygen-carrying microbubbles loaded with Mn2+-doped carbon dots (MnCDs@O2MBs) to enhance SDT and disrupt biofilms. Through precursor screening and design, MnCDs are engineered to exhibit tailored properties of sonodynamic activity and enzyme-like catalytic capabilities. This system provides a dual oxygen supply for amplifying the SDT effects: MnCDs, serving as a sonosensitizer, also chemically convert excess H2O2 at infection sites into oxygen, while the O2MBs physically release oxygen through ultrasound-induced cavitation. The cavitation effect also disrupts biofilms, improving the delivery of sonosensitizers and boosting SDT efficacy. In a diabetic wound model, this strategy downregulated TLR, NF-κB, and TNF inflammatory pathways, reduced pro-inflammatory factor secretion, promoted angiogenesis, and accelerated wound healing, thereby acting as a promising treatment approach for diabetic wound healing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of MnCDs and MnCDs@O2MBs. (a) TEM images of MnCDs (inset: particle size distribution of MnCDs), scale bar: 5 nm; (b) HRTEM images of MnCDs, scale bar: 2 nm; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of free CDs and MnCDs in water media; (d) FT-IR spectra of MnCDs, NHS-Biotin and MnCDs-Biotin; (e) CLSM images of MnCDs@O2MBs, scale bar: 2 μm; (f) Hydrodynamic diameters of MnCDs@O2MBs; (g) ζ potentials of MnCDs, free O2MBs and MnCDs@O2MBs.

In vitro ROS generation performance of dual oxygen supply system. (a) Schematic illustration of ultrasound triggered dual oxygen supply system through both physical and chemical pathway by MnCDs@O2MBs; (b) O2 production curves during a 10-min reaction with 10 mmol L−1 H2O2 under different treatments (inset: generated O2 bubbles in the mixture of MnCDs@O2MBs and H2O2 under ultrasound irradiation); (c) Concentration; (d) Time and; (e, f) Different treatment-dependent 1O2 production; examined by recording the SOSG probe intensity at 525 nm; (g) Degradation of MB by ·OH generated under different treatments; (h) Fluorescence images of S. aureus with different treatments showing ROS production, scale bar: 50 μm; (i) Corresponding statistical data of fluorescence intensity for ROS production. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3 in each group). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001, ns represents no significant difference).

In vitro antibacterial activity of MnCDs@O2MBs. (a) Images of S. aureus colonies on agar plates after different treatments; (b) Corresponding statistical data of CFU colonies per milliliter of S. aureus suspension; (c) Bacterial viability assessed by the XTT assay; (d) Representative CLSM images for a Live/Dead bacterial viability assay of S. aureus suspension under different treatments, scale bar: 200 μm; (e) Corresponding statistical data of relative fluorescence intensity for the Live/Dead bacterial viability assay of S. aureus suspension. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3 in each group). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001, ns represents no significant difference).

Ultrasound-trigged cavitation signal and permeation effect from MnCDs@O2MBs. (a) Schematic diagram of the ultrasound exposure and detection system; (b) IC doses calculated for MnCDs@O2MBs and PBS as a function of acoustic pressure; (c) Schematic diagram of the ultrasound triggered cavitation to treat biofilm; (d) CLSM images showing the distribution of different drugs in S. aureus biofilms, with green fluorescence representing bacterial biofilm and red fluorescence representing different treatments, scale bar: 100 μm; (e) Relative intensity analysis of transverses of CLSM images; (f) Corresponding statistical data of fluorescence intensity for the biofilms; (g) Corresponding statistical data of fluorescence intensity for the drugs in the down layer of the biofilms; (h) Corresponding statistical data of permeability distance of MnCDs. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3 in each group). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001, ns represents no significant difference). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

In vitro antibiofilm activity of MnCDs@O2MBs. (a) 3D-CLSM images for a Live/Dead bacterial viability assay of S. aureus biofilms with different treatments, scale bar: 200 μm; (b) and SEM images of S. aureus with different treatments. Red arrows represent the typical distortion of bacterial morphology and green arrows represent the typical dispersal and collapse of biofilms skeleton, scale bar: 2 μm; (c) Crystal violet staining images and corresponding OD values of the S. aureus biofilms after different treatments; (d) Representative immunofluorescence images of S. aureus biofilms with different treatments and then stained by hypoxyprobe; (e) Corresponding statistical data of fluorescence intensity for hypoxyprobe. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3 in each group). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001, ns represents no significant difference). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Diabetic wound healing effects of MnCDs@O2MBs. (a) A schematic illustration depicts the construction of the full-thickness wound biofilm infection model and treatment procedure; (b) Photographs of representative S. aureus infected mice captured at regular intervals during the observation period following various treatments, along with corresponding LB agar plate images showing bacterial colonies counted from the lesion tissues of S. aureus-infected mice on Day 16, scale bar: 5 mm; (c) Simulated wounds trace for different treatments on Day 0, 8, and 16, and (d) the corresponding statistical wound shrinkage rates; (e) Antibacterial efficiency of various treatments as determined by the bacterial colony counts; (f) H&E and Masson staining images of collected skin tissues from different treatment groups on Day 16 post-treatment. Yellow arrows represent inflammatory cells, including lymphocytes, neutrophils, and macrophages; black arrows reflect hair follicles; green arrows indicate fibroblasts; and red arrows stand for new blood vessels; (g) Quantification of the collagen volume fraction for different groups on Day 16. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 5 in each group). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001, ns represents no significant difference). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Protein changes in wound repair upon dual oxygen self-supplied SDT-amplified combined cavitation antibiofilm therapy. (a) Schematic workflow diagram of the proteomic measurement; (b) Differences in protein abundance in diabetic wound tissues treated with dual oxygen self-supplied SDT-amplified combined cavitation antibiofilm and Control group without treatment, depicted as volcano plots; (c) Orthogonal Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis (OPLS-DA) for all samples based on proteins expression measure; (d) Heatmap representing the wound healing related DEPs in diabetic wound tissues treated with MnCDs@O2MBs-mediated SDT and Control group without treatment, (e) GO annotation of the DEPs, classified by biological process; (f) KEGG enrichment analysis scatter plot; (g–l) GSEA analyses based on GO annotation and KEGG pathway; (m) The PPI network of the top 10 hub-gene measured by Cytohubba, a plug-in for Cytoscape. The shade of color represents the index ranked by the MCC algorithm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Vascular reconstruction and anti-inflammatory efficacy of MnCDs@O2MBs-mediated wound healing. (a) Ultrasound images in B-mode on Day 4 and Doppler-mode of granulation tissues on Day 4, 8 and 16; (b) Corresponding statistical data of blood flow area ratios for ultrasound images; (c) Micrographs of immunohistochemical staining of CD31 in granulation tissues on Day 4, 8 and 16, scale bar: 100 μm; (d) Corresponding statistical data of percentage of CD31 nuclear staining positive cells; (e) Serum concentration of TNF-α were determined by ELISA assay; (f) Serum concentrations of IL-1β were determined by ELISA assay; (g) Serum concentrations of IL-6 were determined by ELISA assay. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 5 in each group). (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001, ns represents no significant difference).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of MnCDs@O2MBs with dual oxygen supply capability for enhanced SDT in diabetic wound healing.

head.ce:title: Dual oxygen supply system of carbon dot-loaded microbubbles with acoustic cavitation for enhanced sonodynamic therapy in diabetic wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diabetic wounds present significant treatment challenges due to their complex microenvironment, marked by persistent inflammation from bacterial infections, hypoxia caused by diabetic microangiopathy, and biofilm colonization. Sonodynamic therapy (SDT) offers potential for treating such wounds by targeting deep tissues with antibacterial effects, but its efficacy is limited by hypoxic conditions and biofilm barriers. To overcome these obstacles, we developed a novel approach using oxygen-carrying microbubbles loaded with Mn2+-doped carbon dots (MnCDs@O2MBs) to enhance SDT and disrupt biofilms. Through precursor screening and design, MnCDs are engineered to exhibit tailored properties of sonodynamic activity and enzyme-like catalytic capabilities. This system provides a dual oxygen supply for amplifying the SDT effects: MnCDs, serving as a sonosensitizer, also chemically convert excess H2O2 at infection sites into oxygen, while the O2MBs physically release oxygen through ultrasound-induced cavitation. The cavitation effect also disrupts biofilms, improving the delivery of sonosensitizers and boosting SDT efficacy. In a diabetic wound model, this strategy downregulated TLR, NF-κB, and TNF inflammatory pathways, reduced pro-inflammatory factor secretion, promoted angiogenesis, and accelerated wound healing, thereby acting as a promising treatment approach for diabetic wound healing.

Mn2+-doped carbon dots, tailored for sonodynamic activity and enzyme-like catalytic capabilities, were conjugated with oxygen microbubbles (O2MBs) to form MnCDs@O2MBs. This system provides a dual oxygen supply through both physical and chemical pathways. Additionally, the acoustic cavitation effect of O2MBs enhances sonosensitizer penetration and antibacterial action, effectively addressing hypoxic conditions and amplifying the effects of sonodynamic therapy for diabetic wound healing.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetic wounds impact approximately 18.6 million individuals globally each year, with at least one-quarter of these cases proving unhealable, thereby increasing the risk of amputation [1,2]. Approximately 50 % of diabetic wounds are further complicated by infections [3], which causes the diabetic wound to remain in the inflammatory phase, thus seriously hindering the process of wound healing [4]. Biofilms act as robust physical and metabolic shields that prevent antibacterial agents from penetrating [5], leading to sustained infections and inflammation in diabetic wounds, which in turn diminish the efficacy of antibacterial treatments. Additionally, biofilms exacerbate the chronic hypoxic conditions caused by diabetic microangiopathy damage [6,7], which impedes angiogenesis and further delays the healing process in diabetic wounds. This pathological hypoxia not only hampers the natural antimicrobial activity of neutrophils but also diminishes the effectiveness of both conventional and novel bactericidal treatments such as antibiotics [5,8] and photodynamic/sonodynamic therapy (PDT/SDT) [9–11]. Consequently, there is a critical need for an oxygen delivery method that can mitigate the hypoxia resulting from microangiopathy and biofilm presence at diabetic wound sites, aiming to enhance sterilization efficacy and hasten wound healing.

SDT has been identified as an effective antibacterial approach for managing bacterial infections by utilizing ultrasound to activate sonosensitizing agents that generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) without the limitation by the depth of tissue penetration [12–14]. These ROS are lethal to bacteria, disrupting the integrity of the bacterial cell wall and membrane and causing severe oxidative stress [15–17], while avoiding issues of drug resistance [18–20]. However, the efficacy of ROS is constrained by the limited oxygen availability in chronic diabetic wounds. Moreover, the efficiency of sonosensitizer delivery is restricted by biofilms that act as barriers, blocking access to the core of infections [21,22]. Thus, the development of advanced nanosystems that can efficiently transport sonosensitizers to infection sites and overcome hypoxia-related challenges offers a promising method to boost the effectiveness of SDT.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterial, have captured significant interest for their roles as nanoprobes, carriers, and reagents [23–25]. The synthesis of CDs is both straightforward and economical, yet they exhibit outstanding physicochemical attributes such as excellent biocompatibility, minimal toxicity, adjustable hydrophilicity, tunable fluorescent emission performance, and abundant surface functional groups [26,27]. These properties herald broad potential applications in the antibacterial domain [23,28]. Moreover, recent studies have highlighted the enzyme-like catalytic capabilities of CDs [29,30], particularly those doped with metals [31–33], which have been shown to mimic catalase activities. Considering these properties and our research foundation in CDs, this project aims to develop metal-doped CDs that not only function as sonosensitizers but also chemically catalyze the breakdown of hydrogen peroxide to release oxygen. By producing oxygen via chemical reactions, this approach seeks to enhance the impact of SDT by combining antibacterial activity with an intrinsic O2 supply, thereby boosting antibacterial effectiveness.

The cavitation effect of ultrasound contact microbubbles (MBs) has been found to effectively remove biofilm barriers, especially in the field of dentistry and medical implants [5,34,35]. By changing the filling gas inside the microbubbles, different types of gases such as oxygen (O2) [36], nitric oxide (NO) [37], hydrogen (H2) [38] and xenon (Xe) [39], which can be transported to the lesion site for therapeutic purposes [40]. In this study, MnCDs are engineered to demonstrate tailored sonodynamic activity and enzyme-like catalytic capabilities, while MnCDs@O2MBs are prepared using a biotin-avidin linkage. This system combines the ability of microbubbles to transport and release oxygen with the in situ oxygen generation by MnCDs, addressing the hypoxic conditions common in chronic diabetic wounds (Scheme 1 ). This dual oxygen supply strategy both physically loading O2 and chemically generating it aims to enhance the antibacterial efficacy of SDT. Additionally, the cavitation effect of MnCDs@O2MBs helps to overcome the limited diffusion of antibacterial agents through biofilms, improving their distribution and enhancing their antibacterial action. This integrated approach combining dual oxygen supply, SDT amplification, and cavitation-facilitated antibiofilm therapy, is designed to accelerate wound healing. Proteomic analysis of treated wound tissue revealed significant improvements, as this strategy effectively controlled bacterial infections, reduced inflammation by inhibiting key inflammatory pathways and pro-inflammatory cytokines, promoted angiogenesis and keratinocyte differentiation, improved the skin barrier, and ultimately facilitated the healing of chronic diabetic wounds.

All chemicals were obtained from commercial sources and used without further purification. Polyethyleneimine (PEI, molecular weight: 600) was acquired from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutathione (GSH), Manganese chloride (MnCl2) and sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3) were acquired from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1,2-dihexadecanoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DPPC), 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy (polyethylene glycol)-2000] (DSPE-PEG2000), and 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[biotinyl (polyethylene glycol)-2000] (DSPE-PEG2000-biotin) were obtained from Xi'an ruixi Biological Technology Co. Ltd. (Shaanxi, China). DCFH-DA was purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). LIVE/DEAD BacLight Bacterial Viability and XTT (2,3-bis (2-methoxy-4-nitro-5-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium-5-carboxanilide) Proliferation Assay kits were purchased from Invitrogen (Waltham, MA). The Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) reagent was acquired from MCE (NJ, USA). Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kits were purchased from ThermoFisher (MA, USA).

The synthesis of MnCDs involves slightly modified solvothermal methods previously reported in the literature. Initially, 1 mmol of reduced glutathione (GSH), 1 mmol of anhydrous MnCl₂, and 0.5 mmol of PEI-600 were dissolved in 10 mL of formamide and thoroughly mixed using ultrasound. This solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave, where it was maintained at 160 °C for 4 h. To eliminate large impurities, the solution was diluted threefold with deionized water and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter. The solution was then purified via dialysis against water for 7 days (MWCO of 3500 Da). Finally, the MnCDs powder was collected through lyophilization. For the preparation of MnCDs-Biotin, MnCDs and NHS-biotin were combined in a molar ratio of 1:80 in a Na2CO3/NaHCO3 buffer solution (pH 9). After thorough dissolution, the mixture was stirred at 37 °C for 6 h. The resulting solution was purified through dialysis against water for 1 day (MWCO of 3500 Da). The MnCDs-Biotin powder was ultimately collected by lyophilization.

DPPC, DSPE-PEG2000, and DSPE-PEG2000-biotin were combined in chloroform in a molar ratio of 82:9:9. The solution was then evaporated using a rotary evaporator, reducing the pressure to 0 mbar for 2 h to completely eliminate the chloroform, resulting in the formation of a lipid film. Next, 5 mL of a 0.05 % glycerol/PBS solution was added to the lipid film, and the mixture was subjected to ultrasonic shaking at 37 °C for 10 min to obtain a lipid solution. In a sealed glass bottle, the solution was extracted and released three times, replacing the upper air in the bottle with a mixture of octafluoropropane (C3F8) and O2 (in a 1:1 vol ratio). This mixture was then agitated using a silver mercury mixer for 30 s to produce O2MBs. The O2MBs were subsequently stirred with avidin and washed three times with PBS (300 g, 3 min) before being centrifuged to obtain O2MBs-avidin. Finally, O2MBs-avidin and MnCDs-Biotin were mixed together, washed with PBS, and centrifuged three times (300 g, 3 min) to yield MnCDs@O2MBs.

The morphological characteristics of MnCDs were analyzed using a JEM-2100F electron microscope. The hydrodynamic sizes and zeta potential of the MnCDs@O2MBs particles were assessed with a Malvern Zetasizer Nanoseries instrument (Nano ZS90, Malvern, England). Absorbance spectra were analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Tecan Spark, Tecan, Switzerland). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi to evaluate the valence states of various elements. Additionally, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) and PL decay measurements were conducted using the iHR 550 fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, USA).

Oxygen production performance was characterized using a dissolved oxygen meter (JPSJ-606L, INESA Scientific Instrument CO., Ltd, China) in a relatively closed measurement environment. The following solvents were added to a 10 mmol L−1 H2O2 solution: (1) ddH2O, (2) O2MBs, (3) MnCDs, and (4) MnCDs@O2MBs. This mixture was allowed to react under ultrasound irradiation (0.5 W cm−2, duty cycle 50 %, 1 MHz, Sonicator 740, Mettler Electronics, USA). The oxygen content in the mixed solutions was recorded throughout the reaction.

Singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG, 5 μmol L−1, Meilune, MA0326, China) was employed as a selective fluorescence probe for detecting singlet oxygen (1O2). Different concentrations of MnCDs@O2MBs and various solutions were diluted in ddH2O for specific purposes. To establish a hypoxic environment, nitrogen was used to remove oxygen from these solutions as needed. After adding the SOSG solution, the mixtures were subjected to ultrasound under consistent conditions (1.0 W cm−2, duty cycle 50 %, 1 MHz). The fluorescence of the mixtures was subsequently measured using a multiscan spectrometer (Tecan Spark, Tecan, Switzerland). The corresponding fluorescence emission intensity was recorded at 525 nm.

For the determination of bacterial ROS production, a co-culture of bacterial suspension with drugs was established to achieve a final MnCDs concentration of 50 μg mL−1 (MnCDs@O2MBs concentration of 1.5 × 107 MB mL−1, same in other experiments). After 10 min, ultrasound treatment (0.5 W cm−2, duty cycle 50 %, 1 MHz, 5 min, Sonicator 740, Mettler Electronics, USA) was applied, or no treatment was administered based on the grouping. Following treatment, the processed bacterial suspensions were mixed with 10 μmol L−1 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and incubated for 20 min. The bacterial precipitates were then collected by centrifugation and washed three times with PBS to eliminate excess fluorescent probes. The fluorescence intensity of the samples was assessed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

This study utilized Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 25923) to assess the antibacterial efficacy of MnCDs@O2MBs, evaluated using the plate count method. A suspension of bacteria (106 CFU mL−1) was prepared at a volume of 200 μL and mixed with various solutions followed by a 10-min incubation period. Subsequently, samples in the ultrasound treatment group underwent ultrasound exposure under controlled conditions (1.0 W cm−2, duty cycle 50 %, 1 MHz) for 5 min. Afterward, 100 μL of the suspension was uniformly applied to agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Finally, the bacterial colonies on the plates were counted and photographed. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XTT bacterial proliferation and bacterial toxicity testing
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the bacteria were incubated in various solutions for 10 min. Samples in the ultrasound treatment group were then subjected to ultrasound under the same parameters (1.0 W cm−2, duty cycle 50 %, 1 MHz) for 5 min. After washing with PBS three times, bacterial precipitates were collected and resuspended in 100 μL of PBS within 96-well plates. Next, 20 μL of XTT solution was added to each well. The samples were incubated for 1 h at 37 °C, protected from light, and the absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a multiscan spectrum (Tecan Spark, Tecan, Switzerland).

For live/dead bacteria staining analysis, SYTO 9 (λex/λem = 480 nm/500 nm) and propidium iodide (PI) (λex/λem = 490 nm/635 nm) were employed. Initially, bacteria were incubated with various solutions for 10 min. Samples in the ultrasound treatment group were exposed to ultrasound under the same conditions (1.0 W cm−2, duty cycle 50 %, 1 MHz) for 5 min. Prior to imaging using fluorescence microscopy or a 3D-CLSM, all bacteria were washed three times with PBS and stained with an equal volume mixture of SYTO 9 (5 μmol L−1) and PI fluorescence probes (30 μmol L−1) for 15 min at 37 °C.

To prepare bacterial biofilms, a single colony from a Luria–Bertani (LB) solid culture medium was selected and inoculated into a liquid LB culture medium, followed by overnight incubation at 37 °C with shaking. The overnight bacterial culture was diluted with fresh liquid LB culture medium and continued to shake until reaching the logarithmic growth phase. Once in this phase, the bacterial solution was added to a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C, with the culture medium replaced every 24 h. After 48 h, the culture medium was carefully removed, and the wells were washed three times with PBS to isolate the biofilms adhered to the 96-well plates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of drug permeation in biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: After harvesting the biofilms, they were treated based on the experimental groups with various solutions for 10 min. The samples assigned to the ultrasound treatment group were exposed to ultrasound under the same conditions (1.0 W cm−2, duty cycle 50 %, 1 MHz) for 5 min. The biofilms were then stained with the SYTO 9 fluorescent probe (5 μmol L−1) for 15 min at 37 °C. Fluorescence imaging was performed using a 3D-CLSM (SYTO 9, λex/λem = 480 nm/500 nm; MnCDs, λex/λem = 540 nm/680 nm), capturing both transverse and longitudinal sections of the biofilms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of in vitro anti-biofilm effect
ce:section.ce:para: Crystal violet (CV) staining was used to assess the in vitro anti-biofilm effect. In brief, biofilms were air-dried at 37 °C for approximately 30 min, followed by fixation with methanol for 15 min. After drying, the biofilms were stained with 5 % (w/v) CV for 15 min at 37 °C. Excess CV was then carefully removed, and the wells were gently washed three times with PBS. The CV absorbed by the biofilms was dissolved in anhydrous ethanol, and its concentration was quantified by measuring the absorbance at 590 nm using a multiscan spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro assessment of hypoxia status in biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Pimonidazole hydrochloride was utilized as the active ingredient (HP2 Hypoxyprobe TM-1 Plus Kit) to detect the in vitro hypoxic status in biofilms. After harvesting the biofilms, they were treated according to different groupings with various solutions 10 min. The samples in the ultrasound treatment group were exposed to ultrasound under consistent conditions (1.0 W cm−2, duty cycle 50 %, 1 MHz) for 5 min. The nanoparticles were then washed off with PBS three times. Following this, pimonidazole hydrochloride (100 μmol L−1) was added to the biofilm for 1 h. The primary antibodies FITC-MAb1 (diluted at 1:100) were added to the biofilm for 1 h, and the biofilms were washed three times with PBS to remove excess antibody. Finally, all biofilm slices were observed under a 3D-CLSM (λex/λem = 488 nm/525 nm).

NIH/3T3 murine fibroblasts were seeded at a density of 8 × 103 cells per well in 100 μL of DMEM within 96-well plates. Following a 24-h incubation, the cells underwent the designated treatments. Next, 100 μL of a 10 % CCK-8 reagent, prepared with DMEM, was added to each well. The cells were incubated for an additional 30 min. Afterward, a microplate reader measured the absorbance at 450 nm to assess cellular viability.

All animal care and treatment procedures complied with the “Guidelines for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals in China” and received approval from the Animal Laboratory of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang University (permit NO. 2021-118). Male C57BL/6J mice were housed in a facility maintained at 23 ± 1 °C with 50 ± 5 % relative humidity, under a 12-h light/dark cycle, and had unrestricted access to water and a standard chow diet. To induce the high-fat diet (HFD)/streptozotocin (STZ) model, 6-week-old mice were subjected to a HFD for 8 weeks, followed by daily intraperitoneal injections of streptozotocin (40 mg kg−1) for 7 consecutive days. Continuous monitoring occurred for one week, identifying mice with blood glucose levels between 15.0 and 28.0 mmol L−1, accompanied by signs of polydipsia, polyuria, and polyphagia, as diabetic for subsequent experiments. To create a full-thickness wound model, the mice were anesthetized with 3 % isoflurane. Before the excision, hair on the hind legs was shaved with an electric clipper and depilatory cream. The skin was then rinsed with alcohol, and a full-thickness knife injury approximately 10 mm in diameter was made on the hind legs, followed by the application of a suspension of S. aureus (109 CFU mL−1) to the wound site. The establishment of the S. aureus subcutaneous biofilm infection model was confirmed after 48 h. Digital photographs were captured on the day of surgery and every four days thereafter, with the camera mounted on a fixed-height frame to maintain a consistent distance from the mice. The relative wound area was calculated using the formula: (wound area on day 0 - wound area on day X)/wound area on day 0 × 100 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of in vivo revascularization effect
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrasound examination to evaluate revascularization was conducted using a Mindray Resona R9 (Mindray Medical System Corporation, Mindray, China) with an L33-8U high-frequency (33 MHz) linear array transducer. Mice were anesthetized, and B-mode and Doppler dual-mode ultrasound imaging was performed, with images recorded at the wound sites.

Serum from the mice was collected at the end of the experiment to measure the levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Detection was carried out using ELISA kits for TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β, in accordance with the manufacturer's guidelines.

The services for proteomics sequencing were conducted by Lianchuan Biotechnology (Hangzhou, China). The procedures included protein extraction, protease digestion, TMT/iTRAQ labeling, HPLC separation, liquid-phase tandem mass spectrometry, database searching, and bioinformatics analysis on tissues collected from the control group and the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group. Differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) were identified according to the criteria of fold change greater than 1.2 or less than 0.83, with a significance level of p < 0.05.

Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 8 software. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and t-tests were employed to assess statistical significance between different treatment groups (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001, and ns indicates no significant difference).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MnCDs and MnCDs@O2MBs
ce:section.ce:para: The MnCDs enriched with amino groups were synthesized using a solvothermal method involving GSH, MnCl2, and PEI in formamide. The morphology of these MnCDs was initially examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the MnCDs were monodispersed nanoparticles with an average particle size of 2.19 ± 0.38 nm. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images revealed lattice fringes of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b), consistent with the (100) facet of graphene, indicating the presence of graphite-like structures. The UV–vis absorption spectra for both the CDs and MnCDs demonstrated similar absorption characteristics, with distinct bands reaching into the near-infrared (NIR) region, as illustrated in Fig. 1c.

We then biotinylated the MnCDs by establishing covalent ester linkages through the interaction of N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) with the free amino groups (NH2) on the MnCDs, enabling their attachment to O2MBs-avidin. To confirm the successful biotinylation, we analyzed FT-IR spectra of the MnCDs. The FT-IR spectrum, depicted in Fig. 1d, showed characteristic peaks around 3400 cm−1, attributed to the N–H stretching vibrations from the abundant amino groups on the surface of MnCDs. The FT-IR spectrum of NHS-Biotin shows characteristic peaks at 1788 cm−1 (carbonyl stretch of NHS), 1704 cm−1 (carbonyl stretch of NHS), and 1215 cm−1 (C–N–C stretch of NHS), respectively. The presence of these peaks in the combined FT-IR spectrum confirmed the successful attachment of NHS-Biotin to the MnCDs, indicating the coexistence of both components. To verify the catalytic function of MnCDs, XPS was employed to examine the status of manganese within the prepared MnCDs (Fig. S1). The Mn 2p XPS spectra indicated the presence of Mn2+ (peaks found at 640.6 eV and 652.4 eV) and Mn4+ (peaks at 642.3 eV and 653.95 eV). The fluorescence spectrum of MnCDs (Fig. S2) shows a deep-red emission at 680 nm.

The band structure of sonosensitizers plays a crucial role in determining their ability to generate ROS under external stimulation. To evaluate the energy band of MnCDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra were analyzed to calculate the bandgap energy (Eg). Using the Tauc method (Fig. S3a), the bandgap of MnCDs was determined to be 1.61 eV. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed on electrodes coated with MnCDs (Fig. S3b), and the conduction band energy (ECB) was estimated to be −1.03 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl). Based on this value, the valence band energy (EVB) was calculated using the equation EVB = ECB + Eg, yielding an EVB of 0.58 eV. An energy-band diagram of MnCDs was constructed based on these findings (Fig. S3c). Furthermore, time-resolved PL spectra were recorded to determine the carrier lifetime of the sonosensitizers. The time-resolved PL spectra for the MnCDs revealed photoluminescence decay with an average lifetime of 4.89 ns (Fig. S4).

The O2MBs used in this study were synthesized according to our previous experience with slight modifications. Instead of using pure oxygen, we incorporated a mixture of O2 and C3F8 to enhance the stability of the MBs (Fig. S5). The biotinylated MnCDs were then attached to these O2MBs using a biotin-avidin connection, with confirmation of this assembly provided by CLSM(Fig. 1e). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to define the particle size and the mean hydrodynamic of MnCDs@O2MBs was found to be around 1581 ± 408.8 nm (Fig. 1f). The Zeta potential exhibited a positive shift from −25.1 mV for the free O2MBs to −21.9 mV for the MnCDs@O2MBs, indicating the remarkable stability of the platform (Fig. 1g). To further explore its stability under different conditions, DLS measurements were performed on samples stored at 4 °C and room temperature (25 °C) over Days 0, 1 and 3. As shown in Fig. S6, no significant changes in hydrodynamic diameter were observed over time, and the corresponding photographs of the dispersions indicated negligible variations in their dispersibility. These findings confirm the excellent stability of MnCDs@O2MBs under conventional storage conditions. To quantify the MnCDs loaded onto the O2MBs, a concentration-dependent standard curve for the MnCDs was created (Fig. S7). The loading rate of MnCDs was determined by measuring the amount of MnCDs that remained unbound post-centrifugation. For a mixture of 107 MBs with 100 μg of MnCDs, the final MnCDs content in the product was calculated to be 362.16 μg/108 MBs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro ROS generation performance of dual oxygen self-supplied system
ce:section.ce:para: Mn2+ is widely known to possess catalase-like activity, as shown in the following reactions: Mn2+ + H2O2 → 2H + MnO2 (1), MnO2 + H2O2 + 2H+ → Mn2+ + 2H2O + O2↑ (2). Leveraging the catalytic properties of MnCDs in chemical pathways and their reliable capacity to store and release O2 via ultrasound in physical pathways, a dual pathway collaborative O2 supply is possible (Fig. 2 a). This design is particularly suited for enhancing SDT in bacterial infection microenvironments like biofilms, which are typically hypoxic but have high levels of H2O2 [41]. The oxygen-releasing capabilities of MnCDs@O2MBs were then tested by monitoring the dissolved O2 concentration in a glass vial containing 10 mM H2O2, with measurements taken over time during ultrasound exposure. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, O2 release from the MBs occurred rapidly, as evidenced by the steep slope of the ΔO2 concentration curve during the first minute. Under ultrasound stimulation, O2MBs quickly release their stored oxygen within the initial 3–4 min. In contrast, MnCDs exhibit a stable O2 generation process due to their catalase-like activity, resulting in a gentler ΔO2 concentration curve with a prolonged duration. When combined, the O2 production curve reflects both rapid release and sustained generation, confirming the effectiveness of MnCDs@O2MBs as a viable O2 source in environments with excess H2O2. 1O2 generation properties of MnCDs@O2MBs were extensively evaluated using SOSG as a sensitive fluorescence indicator. Fig. 2c and d demonstrate that increasing the concentration of MnCDs and the duration of ultrasound exposure both enhance 1O2 production, suggesting that MnCDs are effective sonosensitizers for antibacterial SDT. Subsequent experiments assessed 1O2 generation under various conditions. As shown in Fig. 2e and f, MnCDs alone are less effective as sonosensitizers in the absence of H2O2 and under hypoxic conditions. However, in the presence of H2O2, 1O2 is still produced, even in low-oxygen environments, through the self-catalytic generation of oxygen (Fig. S8). When MnCDs are combined with O2MBs, the production of 1O2 is significantly enhanced, regardless of whether the environment is hypoxic or normoxic.

Mn2+ is also known to convert H2O2 into toxic hydroxyl radicals (·OH) through a Fenton-like reaction, as documented in the literature [42–44]. To verify whether our MnCDs can perform the Mn2+-catalyzed Fenton-like reaction, we employed methylene blue (MB), which degrades in the presence of ·OH, as a marker for hydroxyl radical production. The results indicated a progressive decrease in MB absorbance with increasing MnCDs concentration, suggesting the generation of the ·OH (Fig. S9). Under different treatments, both MnCDs@O2MBs alone and in combination with ultrasound irradiation showed a notable degradation effect on MB, as did MnCDs when similarly irradiated (Fig. 2g).

We further detected the production of ROS in bacteria by incubation with fluorescent probe DCFH-DA. Fig. 2h and i show that negligible fluorescence was observed in the control, ultrasound alone, and O2MBs with US groups. However, a slight fluorescence increase was noted with the addition of MnCDs@O2MBs alone, confirming ROS generation via the Mn2+-catalyzed Fenton-like reaction. Notably, significant fluorescence was detected in S. aureus following US irradiation in both the MnCDs and MnCDs@O2MBs groups, indicating effective ROS production, including 1O2 and ·OH, facilitated by MnCDs as a sonosensitizer. The fluorescence levels were substantially higher in the MnCDs@O2MBs group compared to MnCDs alone, demonstrating that the oxygen supplied by the dual O2 system can significantly enhance the SDT effects of MnCDs. This suggests that MnCDs@O2MBs could provide superior bactericidal effects in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of MnCDs@O2MBs
ce:section.ce:para: Given the proficient oxygen delivery and robust ROS generation capacities of MnCDs@O2MBs, we explored their use as a sonosensitizer for eliminating bacteria via ultrasound irradiation. For this investigation, S. aureus, a bacterium frequently found in diabetic chronic wounds where it forms biofilms, was selected as the model organism.

Firstly, we used the plate counting method to determine the bacterial survival rate and assess the bactericidal efficacy of MnCDs@O2MBs under ultrasound irradiation (Fig. 3 a and b). The analysis of colony counts across various treatment groups revealed that, in comparison to the control group, the cavitation effect alone did not significantly reduce bacterial counts. However, the SDT achieved by MnCDs@O2MBs and MnCDs with ultrasound achieved antibacterial efficiencies of over 50 % and 70 %, respectively. By integrating MnCDs with O2MBs for combined delivery, the SDT impact was enhanced through O2 release from the MBs under ultrasound, achieving more than 95 % antibacterial efficiency, demonstrating the effectiveness of our dual oxygen-self supplied strategy. XTT assays corroborated these findings regarding bacterial viability (Fig. 3c). Live/dead detection using fluorescent dyes SYTO 9 and PI, observed by CLSM (Fig. 3d and e), indicated that the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group had the highest bacterial mortality rate (the lowest relative fluorescence intensity), superior to MnCDs@O2MBs and MnCDs + US groups.

To elucidate the role of O2 supply in treatment outcomes, we conducted the anti-bacterial experiment under the hypoxia condition created by passing N2. When comparing the therapeutic effects of the same treatment group under hypoxic and normoxic conditions, no significant differences were observed between the control and MnCDs@O2MBs groups. This may be attributed to the facultative anaerobic nature of S. aureus and the principle of the Fenton-like reaction, which is independent to the O2 level. In the MnCDs + US and MnCDs@O2MBs + US groups, although their antimicrobial effects were less pronounced than that under normoxic condition, the oxygen supply via combined chemical and physical pathways significantly enhanced the SDT antibacterial efficacy under hypoxic condition compared to the chemical pathway alone (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cavitation effect and in vitro antibiofilm activity of MnCDs@O2MBs
ce:section.ce:para: The biofilm at infection sites serves as a formidable barrier that impedes the effective penetration of antibiotics, making drug delivery deep into the biofilm a critical challenge for eradication. Cavitation, known for its exceptional ability to break barriers in anti-tumor treatments, has also attracted significant interest for biofilm removal applications. Initially, we determined the inertial cavitation (IC) threshold for the MnCDs@O2MBs. The setup used for these experiments is depicted in Fig. 4 a. The detectable IC threshold is defined operationally as the P- (Peak negative acoustic pressure) level at which the IC dose is statistically increases relative to baseline [45], measured in degassed PBS solution without MnCDs@O2MBs. Upon increasing the acoustic pressure to 70 kPa, the microbubbles demonstrated a stable cavitation phenomenon (Fig. 4b). Since microbubbles naturally float when stationary, the dishes were inverted to ensure full contact between the microbubbles and the biofilm surface, thereby maximizing the cavitation effect, as illustrated in Fig. 4c.

To further assess the impact of ultrasound-induced cavitation on drug permeation through the biofilm, we utilized 3D reconstructions of green fluorescence-labeled S. aureus biofilms, with red fluorescence indicating the presence of MnCDs (Fig. 4d). Intensity analyses clearly demonstrated that the relative distribution of drugs in biofilm (Fig. 4e). By measuring the fluorescence intensity of the biofilms, the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group showed the lowest intensity compared to the other groups (Fig. 4f). Notably, drug fluorescence intensity was most pronounced in the lower layers of the biofilm in the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group, and the penetration distance was also the farthest (Fig. 4g and h). These findings suggest that cavitation effectively disrupts the physical barriers established by the biofilm, thereby enhancing drug permeation into deeper layers and significantly improving anti-biofilm efficacy in conjunction with SDT.

We conducted 3D-CLSM using SYTO 9/PI fluorescence-labeled S. aureus biofilms to observe structural changes under various treatments. As shown in Fig. 5 a, both the control and US groups exhibited bright green fluorescence, indicating intact bacterial biofilms with high survival rates. In the MnCDs@O2MBs group, the biofilm remained dense, though some dead bacteria, caused by toxic ·OH, were observed. In the O2MBs + US group, the biofilm structure was damaged, with scattered green fluorescence and occasional red fluorescence indicating dead bacterial cells. The MnCDs + US group displayed widespread bacterial death due to the cytotoxic effects of SDT. Most notably, the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group showed scattered green fluorescence and the most prominent red fluorescence, attributed to cavitation-enhanced MnCDs penetration and the amplified SDT effect from the dual oxygen self-supply system through both physical and chemical pathways. Additionally, the anti-biofilm effect of MnCDs@O2MBs upon ultrasound irradiation was further assessed through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to observe morphological changes. As shown in Fig. 5b, the biofilm structures in the US and MnCDs@O2MBs groups showed minimal disruption compared to the control group, though the latter displayed dead bacteria with structural damage. In contrast, the O2MBs + US group showed scattered and collapsed biofilm structures (indicated by green arrows), validating cavitation's capacity to break down biofilms. The surfaces of bacteria in both the MnCDs + US and MnCDs@O2MBs + US groups were wrinkled and distorted (highlighted by red arrows), with the biofilm completely disintegrated in the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group. For quantitative analysis of the biofilm, crystal violet (CV), an alkaline dye that binds to negatively charged surface molecules and polysaccharide components of the biofilm's extracellular matrix, was utilized. Absorbance measurements revealed that the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group had the lowest CV absorbance (Fig. 5c), indicating the most significant reduction in viable bacteria and biofilm mass.

Accordingly, the biofilm oxygenation enhancement effects after different treatments were investigated by a hypoxyprobe for immunofluorescence staining (Fig. 5d and e). Biofilms treated with either US or O2MBs alone exhibited bright green fluorescence signals, similar to those observed in the control group, indicating persistent hypoxia. In contrast, the O2MBs + US group displayed a notably diminished hypoxic signal, underscoring the effective oxygen storage and release capabilities of O2MBs when activated by ultrasound. More impressively, the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group showed a significantly reduced hypoxic signal compared to the MnCDs group alone, demonstrating the superior oxygen delivery efficiency of our dual oxygen self-supply system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound disinfection and healing efficacy of MnCDs@O2MBs
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the exceptional antibacterial and antibiofilm capabilities of MnCDs@O2MBs demonstrated in vitro, we proceeded to assess their impact on wound disinfection and healing in HFD/STZ diabetic mice. Before this, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of MnCDs@O2MBs on NIH-3T3 cells, as shown in Fig. S11. Cell viability was maintained within the normal range even at a concentration of 200 μg mL−1, indicating the high biosafety profile of MnCDs@O2MBs.

First, after successfully establishing of diabetic C57BL/6J mice, we created a full-thickness wound model on their right hind legs. We then applied 10 μL of S. aureus suspension (109 CFU mL−1) to the wounds, which developed ulcers and with biofilm infections after 48 h. To verify the effective delivery of MnCDs@O2MBs, an in vivo fluorescence imaging study was conducted. As shown in Fig. S12, prominent fluorescence signals were detected at the wound sites, demonstrating the successful accumulation of MnCDs@O2MBs. The mice were subsequently divided into five groups and received treatments on Day 0, 2 and 4 as follows: (1) Control (PBS buffer), (2) US, (3) O2MBs + US, (4) MnCDs + US, and (5) MnCDs@O2MBs + US. Before each ultrasound session, the wounds were covered with sterile waterproof dressings to prevent contact with the gel, and the dressings were removed after treatment to keep the wounds dry. We photographed and measured the wounds every four days to track healing progress. The overview of the experimental procedure is depicted in Fig. 6 a. Fig. 6b illustrates the wound closure trends of S. aureus-biofilm-infected wounds under different treatments in diabetic mice. The healing dynamics were further illustrated using a contour map (Fig. 6c), and the statistical results of the wound healing ratio were calculated (Fig. 6d). In the control and US groups, wound closure efficiency remained below 50 % until the end of the observation period. The O2MBs + US group displayed similar results, suggesting that oxygen delivery alone was insufficient for effective sterilization and healing. Remarkably, in the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group, the wounds were nearly completely filled with granulation tissue by Day 8, in contrast to the persistent skin defects in the MnCDs + US group. Bacterial cultures extracted from the wounds at the end of the study indicated that the oxygen supplied by O2MBs and produced by Mn2+ in reaction with excess H2O2 in the biofilm significantly enhanced the oxygen levels at the wound site, providing more effective antibacterial SDT and notably aiding wound healing (Fig. 6e).

To gain deeper insights, we conducted histological evaluations of the wound tissues after 16 days using hematoxylin-eosin staining (H&E) and Masson staining (Fig. 6f). The control and US groups exhibited substantial neutrophil and lymphocyte infiltration (indicated by yellow arrows) and incomplete epidermal layers, suggesting severe infection. The O2MBs + US group, while still showing incomplete epidermal repair, had numerous irregularly arranged collagen fibers (green arrows). The MnCDs + US group displayed evidence of neovascularization (red arrows) and fibroblast activity, indicative of gradual new tissue formation. In stark contrast, the H&E images from the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group revealed well-formed epidermal layers and regularly aligned collagen fibrils, along with regenerated follicles (black arrows) and some vasculature (red arrows), pointing to superior antibacterial activity and wound healing compared to the other groups. Collagen volume fraction based on Masson's trichrome staining (Fig. 6g) revealed enhanced collagen deposition in the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group. These findings demonstrate that the dual oxygen self-supply strategy for SDT not only effectively eradicates bacteria in biofilm-infected diabetic wounds but also significantly enhances wound healing, demonstrating the potential of combining hypoxia reversal with cavitation in treating such complex infections.

Furthermore, the biocompatibility of MnCDs@O2MBs was assessed through H&E staining of normal tissues and by monitoring body weight changes in wound-bearing C57BL/6J mice. Major organs such as the heart, kidney, spleen, lung, and liver from the treated mice were examined using H&E staining, revealing no significant inflammatory or pathological damage (Fig. S13). The body weight of each mouse was recorded, mice were generally heavier due to high-fat feeding, but no statistically significant differences were found between these five groups. (Fig. S14). These results indicate that MnCDs@O2MBs are a biocompatible nanoplatform suitable for antibacterial therapy applications. To evaluate the safety of US application, H&E staining was performed on the skin tissue surrounding the wound. As illustrated in Fig. S15, histological observation of the skin tissue surrounding the wound revealed the presence of normal skin structures, including the epidermis (red arrows), stratum basale (blue arrows), dermis (green arrows), hypodermis (yellow arrows), and hair follicles (black arrows) in the MnCDs@C3F8MBs + US and MnCDs@O2MBs + US groups. Considering the pharmacokinetic characteristics of ROS, particularly their limited diffusion distance [46] and shorter effective half-life [47] during treatment, we conclude that ROS generated during the antibacterial process are unlikely to cause toxicity to normal tissues nearby.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of wound repair upon the dual oxygen self-supplied SDT-amplified combined with cavitation antibiofilm therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Building on our previous findings, our approach can successfully disrupt bacterial biofilm barriers, enhance the penetration and efficacy of antibacterial agents, and consequently aid in wound repair. However, the underlying mechanisms linking the dual oxygen self-supplied, SDT-amplified, and cavitation-enhanced antibiofilm therapy to wound healing are not yet fully understood. To investigate these mechanisms, we conducted TMT-proteomics on wound tissues. We analyzed five samples each from the control and MnCDs@O2MBs + US groups, with the experimental workflow depicted in Fig. 7 a.

Following treatment with the dual oxygen self-supplied, SDT-amplified, and cavitation-enhanced antibiofilm therapy, we observed significant changes in protein expression, identifying 330 proteins that were upregulated and 420 that were downregulated (Fig. 7b, fold change> 1.20 or <0.83 and p value < 0.05). An OPLS-DA analysis revealed clear distinctions between the two groups in the score plots, showcasing differential protein expression responses to the treatment (Fig. 7c). We conducted a cross-validation of the OPLS-DA model, which demonstrated a Q2 value of −0.0348, indicating high predictive relevance (Fig. S16). These DEPs are visualized in a heatmap in Fig. S17. Among these DEPs, notably, proteins from the keratin (Krt) family, crucial for various healing stages, showed significant upregulation, as did proteins from the filaggrin (Flg) family, important for skin barrier formation. Conversely, inflammatory-related proteins, such as Mmp8, Tlr2 and multiple clusters of differentiation (CD14, CD44, Ptprc/CD45 and CD84), decreased notably (Fig. 7d). Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) analyses were utilized to explore the enrichments of the DEPs potentially involved in wound healing. The GO analysis (Fig. 7e) revealed that the DEPs induced by the treatment were linked to various biological processes, cellular components, and molecular functions. These processes included classical inflammatory responses, angiogenesis, keratinization, and energy metabolism. For cellular components, the DEPs were predominantly associated with the cytosol, cytoplasm, and extracellular space. Regarding molecular functions, the DEPs were mainly involved in identical protein binding, protein-containing complex binding, and actin filament binding (Fig. S18).

KEGG pathway enrichment analysis (Fig. 7f) highlighted significant impact on immune responses to bacterial infection and invasion of epithelial cells. Pathways such as the complement and coagulation cascades, leukocyte transendothelial migration, Fc gamma R-mediated phagocytosis, and neutrophil extracellular trap formation were notably affected. Additionally, critical life cycle and regulatory systems were influenced, including glutathione metabolism, the pentose phosphate pathway, and various amino acid metabolism pathways, particularly glycine metabolism. Glycine, recognized as a potent anti-inflammatory micronutrient [48], has been shown to inhibit NF-κB activation and reduce the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines like TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β, which prolonging the inflammatory response in chronic wounds [49].

To comprehensively analyze the biological functions impacted by the treatment, gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) was conducted on the whole-proteome data. GSEA results indicated that proteins involved in skin development (Fig. 7g), structural constituent of skin epidermis (Fig. S19a) and keratinocyte differentiation (Fig. S19b) are upregulated in the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group. Conversely, several categories associated with processes of inflammatory response (Fig. S19c) and defense response to gram positive bacterium (Fig. S19d) were enriched in control group, indicating a substantial presence of bacteria and an active immune response. It is noteworthy that after treatment, cholesterol metabolism downregulated (Fig. 7h) and branched chain amino acid (BCAA) including valine, leucine and isoleucine degraded (Fig. 7i), which inhibited the TLR (Fig. 7j) signaling and thus reduced the expression of NF-κB (Fig. 7k) and TNF (Fig. 7l) inflammatory signaling pathway respectively [50,51]. This suggests that the metabolic responses involving glycine and cholesterol in wounds treated with MnCDs@O2MBs under ultrasound irradiation were diminished, leading to reduced cytokine secretion and inflammation. This decrease in inflammatory activity was accomplished by downregulating the TLR, TNF, and NF-κB signaling pathways. Thus, by modulating these pathways, the treatment facilitated the shift of wounds from the inflammatory to the proliferative phase, thereby enhancing skin development and keratinocyte differentiation.

The STRING database and Cytoscape (v3.10.2) were utilized to construct protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks for the DEPs identified in the biological processes of the GO enrichment (Fig. S20). Using Cytohubba, a Cytoscape plugin, we pinpointed the hub genes, limiting our focus to the top 10 as ranked by the Maximal Clique Centrality (MCC) algorithm. As illustrated in Fig. 7m, key findings include a decrease in Tlr2, which is linked to reduced activity in the TLR signaling pathway, and declines in Cybb and Mmp8, associated with diminished activity in the TNF signaling pathway. Additionally, a decrease in Ltf is connected to reduced activity in the NF-κB signaling pathway. These findings demonstrate that the dual oxygen self-supplied, SDT-amplified, and cavitation-enhanced treatment effectively curtails the expression of proteins involved in inflammatory signaling pathways, thereby supporting skin development and keratinization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Vascular reconstruction and anti-inflammatory efficacy of MnCDs@O2MBs-mediated wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: Proteomic results indicate that our dual oxygen self-supplied, SDT-amplified, and cavitation-enhanced antibiofilm therapy influences angiogenesis during wound healing. Properly regulated angiogenesis is essential for wound repair as it delivers oxygen and nutrients to rapidly proliferating cells. However, in diabetes, hyperglycemia can lead to abnormal angiogenesis, complicating wound healing. To assess vascular reconstruction during the healing process, ultrasound evaluations of blood flow and IHC staining were performed. Initially, ultrasound was used to monitor overall blood flow status at the wound sites (Fig. 8 a and b). In the control and US groups, a deficient blood supply persisted throughout the experiment. However, in the O2MBs + US and MnCDs + US groups, there was a gradual improvement in blood supply as treatment continued. Remarkably, in the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group, capillary density at the wound site early in treatment was significantly higher than in normal skin, enhancing blood flow and fostering wound healing, in alignment with existing literature [52,53]. As healing progressed and the epidermis fully regenerated, a shift from pro-angiogenic to anti-angiogenic factors occurred, leading to blood vessel regression through apoptosis and a subsequent reduction in blood supply [54].

Wound healing encompasses several stages: hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling. In the proliferation stage, a new microvascular network develops within the granulation tissue to satisfy the nutritional demands of recovering tissue, resulting in a greater density of capillaries in the wound area compared to normal skin. As healing advances into the remodeling phase, non-essential vessels undergo regression and the density of blood vessels decreases as a result of programmed cell death. Following the assessment of blood supply under various treatments, we employed CD31 IHC staining to visualize the vascular reconstruction at the microscopic level within the wound (Fig. 8c and d). CD31, a cell adhesion molecule known for its proangiogenic properties, is commonly used as a biomarker for angiogenesis. In the control and US groups, the wound surface was severely damaged, and by the experiment's end, extensive infiltration of inflammatory cells persisted without evident angiogenesis. In the O2MBs + US group, while the direct oxygen supply from O2MBs initially supported vascular regeneration, the lack of sufficient antibacterial activity hindered effective wound healing. The MnCDs + US group showed a trend in angiogenesis similar to the O2MBs + US group, but the addition of antibacterial SDT activity led to better overall wound recovery. In the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group, the effective and sustained oxygen supply of the dual physical and chemical oxygen delivery system, combined with the antibacterial SDT and cavitation effect, facilitated early angiogenesis (Day 4). As a result, the epidermal layer began to restore at this stage. At the endpoint of the experiment, as the wound had fully healed, non-functional vessels underwent apoptosis, reducing the proangiogenic demand and consequently lowering CD31 expression. This indicated that effective antimicrobial therapy combined with restored oxygen supply allows angiogenesis at the wound site to progress naturally through its physiological stages from promotion to inhibition, thereby significantly enhancing vascular perfusion and tissue healing.

Our therapeutic strategy has been shown to influence glycine metabolism, which in turn affects cytokine levels. To further evaluate the systemic inflammatory response, we analyzed three pro-inflammatory cytokines: TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β. As shown in Fig. 8e–g, the levels of systemic cytokine secretion were significantly higher in the control, US, O2MBs + US, and MnCDs + US groups, indicating an elevated inflammatory response and substantial engagement of host immune cells in wound healing, compensating for their limited anti-infection capabilities. In contrast, the MnCDs@O2MBs + US group exhibited a suppressed systemic inflammatory response due to the robust antibacterial effect of our treatment system. Prolonged inflammation can impede new tissue formation, exacerbating infection and leading to chronic wounds [55]. Integrating these findings, our dual oxygen self-supplied, SDT-amplified, cavitation-enhanced antibiofilm therapy effectively sterilized the wound, altered amino acid metabolism at the site, and reduced both the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines and the activity of classical inflammatory metabolic pathways. This combination facilitated the transition of the wound from the inflammatory phase to the proliferative and remodeling phases, enhancing overall wound healing.

In summary, we developed an innovative anti-biofilm strategy to address the hypoxia commonly found in diabetic wounds, which hinders the efficacy of SDT. By fabricating Mn2+ doped carbon dots with sonosensitive properties and then conjugating with O2MBs, a dual oxygen self-supply system was designed to alleviate hypoxia for enhanced SDT. This system produces oxygen through the reaction of Mn2+ with surplus H2O2 found in the biofilm microenvironment, while oxygen release is also triggered by ultrasound from the O2MBs. In vitro studies demonstrated that MnCDs possess effective sonosensitive properties, and their antibacterial SDT efficacy was further enhanced by the efficient O2 delivery provided by MnCDs@O2MBs. Moreover, the cavitation effect generated by ultrasound activation of the O2MBs facilitated the penetration of the sonosensitizer through biofilm barriers, thereby improving drug delivery. In a diabetic biofilm-infection model, MnCDs@O2MBs not only showed robust antibacterial properties but also promoted angiogenesis and reduced inflammation via the TLR, TNF, and NF-κB pathways, thus speeding up wound healing.

This method provides a promising antibiotic-free strategy to enhance SDT against biofilm-associated infections by integrating dual oxygen self-supply with cavitation effects. By leveraging the unique microenvironment of biofilm infections, this advanced antibacterial system effectively overcomes the barriers posed by biofilms, offering improved treatment for chronic diabetic wounds and presenting potential applications for managing other chronic wounds with concomitant infections.
